answer:
In Mirza Ghalib's *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, *fanaa* (often transliterated as *fana* in the corpus) is a central Sufi motif denoting the annihilation or dissolution of the individual self (*nafs* or ego) into a greater whole—typically the Divine Beloved, love itself, or the universe. This is not mere destruction but an ecstatic merger, often figured through metaphors of natural transience (e.g., a dewdrop evaporating in sunlight or a drop merging into the river), where separation from the beloved's essence yields ultimate bliss or cure.[^1][^2] It recurs as a paradoxical joy amid suffering, love's extremity, or existential erasure, aligning the lover's pain with spiritual union (*baqa* after *fanaa*).[^3]

Ghalib evokes *fanaa* as life's telos: "Our very being proves that we are meant to wane; / Erased, until our own sworn oath is all that will remain," linking existence to its own obliteration.[^4] In divine contemplation, it mirrors dewdrops taught by sunbeams "how to fade," with the poet awaiting a glimpse that consummates his fragile presence.[^5] Before "the Names of Divine Majesty," he vanishes "as dewdrops... in the sun’s bright majesty."[^6]

The motif intensifies in love's despair: pain exceeding limits "become[s] a cure," just as a drop's "true bliss is in the river to become."[^7] Ghalib courts it deliberately—"What sweet despair, to court annihilation's art"—donning life like dust-robe for dissolution.[^8] Ultimately, it unifies chaos: "Oh Ghalib, I see only one path: annihilation's plain; / It is the thread that binds the scattered universe, that which becomes the whole again."[^9]

This theme permeates dozens of ghazals (the Diwan spans 239), blending erotic longing, philosophical irony, and mysticism, but the corpus translations interpret rather than always retain the term *fanaa* literally (favoring "annihilation" or metaphor). Not every instance is exhaustively indexed, but it underscores Ghalib's vision of self-loss as transcendence.[^10]

[^1]: *Mirza Ghalib*, "Ishrat-e-qatra hai darya mein fana ho jaana" (*Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 86), [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/86-ishrat-e-qatra-hai-darya-mein-fana-ho-jaana/translation/#p-a9ff31).

[^2]: Ibid., full chapter [here](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/86-ishrat-e-qatra-hai-darya-mein-fana-ho-jaana/translation/).

[^3]: *Mirza Ghalib*, "Garm-faryaad rakha shakl-e-nihaali ne mujhe" (*Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 46), [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/46-garm-faryaad-rakha-shakl-e-nihaali-ne-mujhe/translation/#p-6b73ab).

[^4]: *Mirza Ghalib*, "Be-e'tedaaliyon se subuk sab mein hum huay" (*Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 18), [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/18-be-etedaaliyon-se-subuk-sab-mein-hum-huay/translation/#p-da73dd).

[^5]: *Mirza Ghalib*, "Aah ko chahiye ik umr asar hote tak" (*Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 3), [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/03-aah-ko-chahiye-ik-umr-asar-hote-tak/translation/#p-9ed8d2).

[^6]: *Mirza Ghalib*, ch. 46 (as in [^3]), [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/46-garm-faryaad-rakha-shakl-e-nihaali-ne-mujhe/translation/#p-6b73ab).

[^7]: *Mirza Ghalib*, ch. 86 (as in [^1]), [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/86-ishrat-e-qatra-hai-darya-mein-fana-ho-jaana/translation/#p-a9ff31).

[^8]: *Mirza Ghalib*, "Loon waam bakht-e-khufta se yak-khwaab-e-khush walay" (*Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 127), [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/127-loon-waam-bakht-e-khufta-se-yak-khwaab-e-khush-wal/translation/#p-2ac76d).

[^9]: *Mirza Ghalib*, "sitaish-gar hai zahid is qadar jis bagh-e-rizwan ka" (*Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 233), [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/233-sitaish-gar-hai-zahid-is-qadar-jis-bagh-e-rizwan-k/translation/#p-13188e).

[^10]: Search results for *fana*/*fanaa* in transliterations yield ~10+ Ghalib chapters (e.g., chs. 3, 18, 46, 86, 127, 233); full Diwan context confirms recurrence.

args.query: "self annihilation" OR annihilation OR extinction OR "ego death" ghalib
fana OR fanaa OR annihilation ghalib
